skill.name: molfeat
skill.description: Guides molecular featurization with molfeat for QSAR, virtual screening, similarity search, and molecular machine learning workflows.
skill.summary: Use molfeat to convert molecules into machine learning features and embeddings.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk hits, but review shows they are overwhelmingly false positives from Markdown code fences, shell installation examples, and chemistry terms such as MACCS and desc2D. No prompt injection, malware intent, credential access, or data exfiltration was found in the reviewed files. Publication is acceptable with a warning because examples demonstrate pickle-based cache loading, which is unsafe for untrusted files.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe Pickle Cache Pattern in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The examples load embeddings with pickle.load from a local cache file. This is not malicious by itself, but pickle can execute code if the cache file is attacker-controlled, so users should only load trusted cache files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in documentation examples and is a known unsafe deserialization risk. Confidence is moderate because the example creates and reloads its own local cache, with no evidence of malicious intent or remote input.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution hits are Markdown inline code, fenced code examples, or package installation instructions. No executable skill hook or dynamic command construction was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visibly documentation syntax or static example commands, not executable code loaded by the skill. The repository contains only Markdown skill files and references in the analyzed file set.

low_findings.title: Chemistry Terminology Triggered Malware Keyword Rules
low_findings.description: High-risk C2 and weak-cryptography findings map to molecular terms such as desc2D, MACCS, descriptors, and model names. These are legitimate cheminformatics identifiers, not command-and-control behavior or cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are plainly molecular featurizer names and descriptor examples. No network beaconing, cryptographic API use, payload staging, or obfuscation was found at these locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The skill lists official documentation, GitHub, PyPI, and tutorial URLs. These are user-facing references and do not cause automatic network requests or data transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as resource links in Markdown. There is no evidence that the skill fetches these URLs or sends user data to them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pickle Deserialization Example
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation demonstrates pickle.load for cached embeddings. This should be treated as a dangerous pattern when cache files can be modified by untrusted users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous API usage is explicit, but the context is a local cache example rather than a confirmed exploit path.

content.user_title: Build Molecular ML Features with Molfeat
content.value_statement: Molecular machine learning needs consistent features from SMILES, RDKit molecules, and compound libraries. This skill guides molfeat workflows for fingerprints, descriptors, pretrained embeddings, and model-ready pipelines.
actual_capabilities: Selects molfeat calculators for ECFP, MACCS, MAP4, RDKit descriptors, Mordred descriptors, and pharmacophore features.
Explains how to use MoleculeTransformer for batch featurization and scikit-learn pipelines.
Guides pretrained molecular embedding workflows using ChemBERTa, ChemGPT, GIN, Graphormer, and related models.
Provides examples for QSAR modeling, virtual screening, similarity search, and chemical space analysis.
Covers performance choices such as parallel processing, chunking, dtype selection, and caching.
Helps handle invalid SMILES and choose fallback strategies for large molecular datasets.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install molfeat or run molecular models by itself.
Some workflows require optional molfeat dependencies, RDKit, PyTorch, DGL, or transformer packages.
Pretrained model downloads may require internet access and substantial local storage.
Generated guidance still needs scientific validation against the target assay, dataset, and model objective.

use_cases.title: Build QSAR Feature Pipelines
use_cases.description: Choose interpretable descriptors or fingerprints and connect them to scikit-learn models for property prediction.

use_cases.title: Screen Compound Libraries
use_cases.description: Prepare fast molecular fingerprints and ranking workflows for large virtual screening projects.

use_cases.title: Compare Molecular Embeddings
use_cases.description: Evaluate handcrafted features against pretrained transformer or graph neural network embeddings.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Featurizer
prompt_templates.prompt: I have SMILES strings for a small QSAR dataset. Recommend molfeat featurizers and explain when to use each option.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Batch Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a molfeat batch featurization workflow for 50,000 molecules with invalid SMILES handling and reproducible settings.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Model Features
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare ECFP, RDKit 2D descriptors, Mordred descriptors, and ChemBERTa embeddings for my molecular property model.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Large Screening
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a scalable virtual screening pipeline using molfeat with chunking, parallel processing, caching, and feature validation checks.

output_examples.input: Recommend features for a binary activity QSAR model with 5,000 SMILES.
output: Start with ECFP for a strong baseline and MACCS for a compact interpretable comparison.
Add RDKit 2D descriptors if the model needs named physicochemical features.
Use cross-validation to compare feature sets before trying slower pretrained embeddings.

output_examples.input: Help me process a million compounds for similarity search.
output: Use fast fingerprints such as ECFP, MACCS, or MAP4 before deep learning embeddings.
Process molecules in chunks and keep invalid SMILES handling enabled.
Store featurizer configuration with the results so the run can be reproduced.

output_examples.input: When should I use ChemBERTa embeddings?
output_examples.output: Use ChemBERTa when transfer learning may capture molecular context beyond fixed fingerprints. Benchmark it against simpler features because it is slower and requires optional dependencies.

best_practices: Start with simple fingerprints or descriptors before using slower pretrained models.
Save featurizer configuration, dependency versions, and preprocessing choices for reproducibility.
Validate feature shapes, missing values, and invalid molecule handling before model training.
anti_patterns: Do not assume a larger descriptor set is better without validation on held-out data.
Do not load pickle caches from untrusted locations or shared directories.
Do not compare featurizers without using the same train-test split and preprocessing rules.

faq.question: Does this skill run molfeat automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples for using the molfeat Python library in your own environment.

faq.question: Which molecular inputs are covered?
faq.answer: The documentation focuses on SMILES strings and RDKit molecule objects, which are standard molfeat inputs.

faq.question: Can it help with pretrained embeddings?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes ChemBERTa, ChemGPT, GIN, Graphormer, MolT5, and dependency choices for those models.

faq.question: Is it suitable for QSAR workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for fingerprints, descriptors, scikit-learn pipelines, cross-validation, and property modeling.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive code?
faq.answer: It is mostly Markdown documentation. One example uses pickle caching and should only be used with trusted local files.

faq.question: Can it replace scientific model validation?
faq.answer: No. It helps build featurization workflows, but assay quality, split strategy, and model validation remain user responsibilities.
